questions.question: What does the speaker say is the modest, stated goal of this video?
options: To refute every Roman Catholic dogma in detail
To provide conceptual clarity about a Protestant view of justification and where it differs from Catholic teaching
To prove the Council of Trent had no historical precedent
To argue that James 2 contradicts Martin Luther
questions.answer: To provide conceptual clarity about a Protestant view of justification and where it differs from Catholic teaching

questions.question: How does the speaker characterize his view of the 1999 Joint Declaration on the Doctrine of Justification?
options: It solved everything between Lutherans and Catholics
It was irrelevant and changed no serious disagreements
A middle view: meaningful progress, but not total resolution of important differences
It was primarily about Eastern Orthodox participation
questions.answer: A middle view: meaningful progress, but not total resolution of important differences

questions.question: According to the speaker, why would the Reformation not be “over” even if Protestants and Catholics fully agreed on justification?
options: Because other issues (authority of the church, Marian dogmas, papal infallibility, etc.) would still divide
Because John Calvin rejected all ecumenical dialogue
Because the Joint Declaration would then be revoked
Because indulgences are the only doctrine that matters historically
questions.answer: Because other issues (authority of the church, Marian dogmas, papal infallibility, etc.) would still divide

questions.question: Timothy George’s phrase the speaker appeals to for patient truth-seeking ecumenism is:
questions.answer: Ecumenism of conviction

questions.question: The speaker says Roman Catholic theology (as official theology) should not be equated with which biblical error?
options: The Galatian Judaizing problem condemned by Paul
questions.answer: The Galatian Judaizing problem condemned by Paul

questions.question: In the speaker’s summary, Catholic teaching ties initial entry into grace/friendship especially to:
options: Meriting justification by moral achievements prior to baptism
Baptism as the ordinary means, not a crude “faith plus works” meriting scheme
Confession to a priest before any grace is received
questions.answer: Baptism as the ordinary means, not a crude “faith plus works” meriting scheme

questions.question: According to the speaker, how does the Catechism of the Catholic Church (paragraph 1989) define justification?
options: Only the remission of sins, with sanctification treated as a separate unrelated topic
Not only remission of sins but also sanctification and renewal of the interior man
questions.answer: Not only remission of sins but also sanctification and renewal of the interior man

questions.question: For most Protestants in this presentation, what is the organizing “hub” with justification as one spoke among others?
questions.answer: Union with Christ

questions.question: Calvin is quoted to say faith alone justifies, yet the faith that justifies is not alone—using an analogy of:
options: The sun’s heat warming the earth while heat is conjoined with light
questions.answer: The sun’s heat warming the earth while heat is conjoined with light

questions.question: Martin Luther is quoted (as summarized) to say works are necessary for salvation but:
options: They replace faith as the instrument of justification
They are irrelevant if faith exists
questions.answer: They do not cause salvation

questions.question: Richard Hooker is cited as saying (in the speaker’s line of quotes):
options: We are justified by works alone
We are justified by faith alone, yet without good works we are not justified
Good works justify before faith exists
questions.answer: We are justified by faith alone, yet without good works we are not justified

questions.question: The speaker uses James 2:19 (“even the demons believe”) to argue James is treating “faith” as:
options: Whole-person trust identical to Paul’s justifying faith
Intellectual assent, not the yielding trust Protestants associate with justifying faith
questions.answer: Intellectual assent, not the yielding trust Protestants associate with justifying faith

questions.question: On James 2, the speaker says James is largely addressing which question?
options: How one is forensically reckoned righteous before God in Paul’s sense
How you recognize who a true Christian is (manifestation/vindication), versus Paul’s different question
Whether circumcision is required for Gentiles
Whether the Law of Moses is abolished
questions.answer: How you recognize who a true Christian is (manifestation/vindication), versus Paul’s different question

questions.question: Which Pauline texts does the speaker highlight for forensic/reckoning language?
questions.answer: Romans 4:5–6 and Galatians 2:16

questions.question: The American vs British “football” analogy is used to illustrate:
options: How mismatched definitions of the same word create false agreements and disagreements
That Catholics reject all sports metaphors
questions.answer: How mismatched definitions of the same word create false agreements and disagreements

questions.question: The speaker’s favorite biblical image for justification (clothing/filthy garments removed) is drawn from:
questions.answer: Zechariah 3

questions.question: Tim Keller is mentioned to support which point?
options: That modern people never seek meaning
That “justification by works” can operate even in secular identity formation
That the Joint Declaration is infallible
questions.answer: That “justification by works” can operate even in secular identity formation

questions.question: Doug Moo is summarized as warning that some New Perspective readings of Romans risk:
options: Swapping background and foreground so corporate concerns overshadow individual believers as Paul’s immediate concern
questions.answer: Swapping background and foreground so corporate concerns overshadow individual believers as Paul’s immediate concern

questions.question: Galatians 3:10 is used to argue “works of the law” cannot mean only:
options: Corporate boundary markers like circumcision, because the curse applies to failing the whole law
questions.answer: Corporate boundary markers like circumcision, because the curse applies to failing the whole law

questions.question: The speaker highlights “formal cause” disagreement as:
options: Imputed/credited righteousness versus infused righteousness and cooperative growth in grace
questions.answer: Imputed/credited righteousness versus infused righteousness and cooperative growth in grace

questions.question: The second major remaining difference the speaker emphasizes after formal cause is:
options: The broader Catholic soteriological context (sacraments, penance, purgatory, indulgences, confession after mortal sin, etc.)
Whether Catholics believe in the Trinity
questions.answer: The broader Catholic soteriological context (sacraments, penance, purgatory, indulgences, confession after mortal sin, etc.)

questions.question: The speaker distinguishes historical novelty charges into:
options: Meaning of justification versus means of justification
Old Testament vs New Testament only
questions.answer: Meaning of justification versus means of justification

questions.question: Jaroslav Pelikan is quoted (from a pre–Vatican II Protestant-vantage book) to argue that:
options: No Catholic ever spoke of grace
The Council of Trent selected one medieval trajectory about justification and elevated it officially, narrowing earlier breadth
questions.answer: The Council of Trent selected one medieval trajectory about justification and elevated it officially, narrowing earlier breadth

questions.question: Which church father does the speaker focus on most extensively to complicate simplistic “sixteenth-century novelty” narratives?
questions.answer: John Chrysostom

questions.question: On Chrysostom’s Romans homilies, the speaker emphasizes language such as “straightway justified” to suggest Chrysostom often treats justification as:
options: Only a lifelong transformative process with no decisive moment
A status received at faith, with a transformed life following as response
questions.answer: A status received at faith, with a transformed life following as response

questions.question: The speaker closes by noting Trent’s canons and anathemas are still “on the table,” while also citing Anselm to show:
options: Devotional depth of sin and refuge in Christ can sound spiritually resonant across Catholic/Protestant lines despite technical disagreements
questions.answer: Devotional depth of sin and refuge in Christ can sound spiritually resonant across Catholic/Protestant lines despite technical disagreements
